[1:01:05] So our pricing signals incentives. Okay. So for us, you know, based on what the price of it is kind of dictates what something uh is going to do. So as an example, like when you buy uh this $41,000 watch, it's like, dude, I'm not fucking you know?
[1:01:25] Like rich people are not dumb. It's like, I'm not spending $41,000 on a fucking stainless steel watch for no reason besides status. It's like, well, I'm doing it because this watch at one point when it first came out was upwards of $70,000.
[1:01:45] So, if I could potentially see, and every watch right now is getting absolutely fucking dunked on, you know, like the value versus what these watches have have historically been in terms of pricing is is so extreme right now and the discrepancies of what they once were worth versus what they're selling for now that it just seems like a great time to buy.
[1:02:10] So based on the price of the product versus what it's historically been, it seems like once again the incentive is clear. Like I have a probability to make money on this and I get to flex status in the meantime. Okay.
[1:02:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Agency Pricing Incentive Example"
NÚMEROS: $15,000/mês + 10% net rev share, 30 dias para resultado
VISUAL: Breakdown de como pricing structure sinaliza incentivos - rev share mostra alinhamento de interesses, prazo de 30 dias para ROI comunica saving time
[1:02:35] When it comes to my ads, it's like well charging $15,000 a month and having a 10% net rev share. What does that signal in terms of my pricing incentive? What signals I'm probable to make you a fuckload of money? And guess what I also tell people based on how it's expressed.
[1:02:55] So, how I break it down is I say, "Well, we're going to come in and we're going to do this within 30 days." Like, we do a month-to-month agreement. So, we understand how the game goes. If we don't make you money within 30 days, I mean, to be clear, like, you're not going to want to work with us.
[1:03:15] So, our game is pretty simple. Like, we come in, we charge you what we charge, we have you spend what we have you spend on ad spend, and dude, like, we got to make you that back within 30 days at least, ideally more, obviously, in order to continue the game.
[1:03:35] So, when I say how I break it down, I'm signaling with my price, I'm intending to make you a lot of money. When I break it down and I specifically say once again, hey, I have to make you back that money within 30 days. Once again, that also signals the saving time aspect of it. Okay?

[1:04:20] Um, we make sure we express when we tell people like, yeah, like people always ask when they buy stuff from us like, "Oh, what are you actually going to do for that 15K?" And we're like, "Listen, I will do whatever it takes to generate you revenue." Okay?
[1:04:40] If I have to build a funnel, I will. If I have to like write some emails, I will. you know, if I have to like consult you on some shit I need you to do, I will. The main thing we do every day is we run the ads, okay?
[1:05:00] We typically start on these channels, you know, just to be clear though, like because I have this rev share, I give a fuck about making you more money than what you're going to spend with us. That's the main thing that I care about.
[1:05:20] So, to be clear, it's like, yeah, technically I can do all these things, but that's not what I'm selling you, you know? I'm I'm selling you the result. Like, that's what I'm specifically selling in this case. Okay.

[1:06:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Moving Company Expectation Failure"
NÚMEROS: $3,500 cotado como "final price", $2,200 adicionais cobrados depois, $600 tip dado, 3 dias de trabalho
VISUAL: História de falha de expectativas - preço "final" quebrado, cobranças surpresa, experiência arruinada mesmo com preço total ainda razoável vs. expectativa inicial de $10k
[1:06:40] So, I recently moved here into the penthouse, okay? And and moving into the penthouse was a breeze. I didn't have to do shit. Obviously, I hired movers, okay? And they packed all my shit. Uh and this is a lot of stuff. So, it was a three-day thing. three-day excursion for the movers.
[1:07:00] Day one was pack everything, load the truck. Day two was bring it all over here. Day three was unpack it all and make it look like you were never here. Okay, they quoted me $3500. And they said, "This is the final price." Okay, in their own words written in an email, final price, 3500 bucks.
[1:07:25] Okay, I honestly was prepared to spend like 10k. That's just kind of what I thought it would cost. But 3,500 bucks, I was like, "Shit, I'll take it." To be clear, that was a great deal, you know. I was pretty happy about that.
[1:07:45] Now, they do the move, everything goes smooth. No issues, you know. I was actually very pleased. I thought it went quite well. Couple days goes by. I Marina gets an email saying that we owed an extra $2,200.
[1:08:05] I was like, "What the fuck the fuck this guy talking about? We owe $2,200 for what?" So, I called the guy up and I'm like, "Hey, just curious. Like, you know, I tipped you guys like 600 bucks. You know, I paid you your 3,500 bucks. Where the fuck are you pulling 2,200 extra dollars out of your ass from?"
[1:08:30] And in like a super shaky, unconfident voice, he says something along the lines of like, "There were some things that were heavier than we anticipated. It took longer than what we expected and I had to use some extra supplies."
[1:08:50] And you know, obviously I just start going through his points and I say things like, "Well, what are you talking about?" Like, "This was the exact timeline that we laid out. [1:09:15] And in terms of extra supplies like what did you get and he was like oh we had to get a mattress cover and some nuts and bolts for one of the TV hanging on the wall so immediately I know he's just trying to fuck me you know.
[1:09:35] He probably saw Ferraris at the other house which he didn't previously see because they were getting worked on probably came over to the penthouse and just realized like oh I could just fuck this guy you know Rich people hate getting fucked like that.
[1:09:55] Even though it's only a little 2,200 bucks, it's like terrible experience. What? To be clear though, remember what I said initially, right? Technically, that would only come out to a total of $5,700, which was still less than what I initially expected to pay.
[1:10:15] But why was I upset about the $2,200? It's the expectation. That's all it is. Expectations are critical when you're selling to rich people. Rich people hate thinking about stuff more than they have to. And that's one big reason the expectations matter.
[1:10:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Agency Expectation Management"
NÚMEROS: 30 dias para resultado, $1M/mês em case studies, 6-12 meses timeline realista para $1M/mês
VISUAL: Gerenciamento de expectativas em vendas - case studies criam expectativa alta, necessidade de quebrar timeline realista, expectativas conservadoras vs. overpromise
[1:10:45] So when I break something down of like, hey, I have to turn this money into more money within 30 days and I fail to do that, you know, I break the expectation that I set and therefore it's seen as a failure.
[1:11:05] Um, one big thing that, you know, I have to make sure I set as an expectation is like, look, we're constantly talking about making a million dollars a month. We when we go over our case studies with people, almost all of them are people who have made millions of dollars or at least a million dollars a month.
[1:11:25] What does the person expect just based on case studies alone? They expect to make a million dollars a month, right? So therefore, from an expectations perspective, I have to break down to them what the timeline is to actually accomplishing that.
[1:11:45] I have to tell them like very clearly, look dude, I mean, in our first 30 days, we might be at like break even or like a two to one rorowaz on our test budget or something, you know, and then we start the process of scaling up.
[1:12:05] Yeah, we've had clients that have hit a million in the first 30 days. We've had clients hit a million in the first 90 days, but most of our million-doll month clients take like somewhere between like six months to a year. That's pretty common.
[1:12:25] So, we really emphasize once again like conservative expectations. We don't try to overpromise and overd deliver. We also don't really try to underpromise. We just try to set conservative expectations. It's all it really comes down to.

[1:14:00] So like when something starts to go wrong and not the way that they expected it, they almost feel like they have to get involved, which is considered like a a high stress event. Okay, their stress bar in terms of like how they operate. If this is if this is like the bar of stress and it's zero to 100, it's like their stress bar is pretty much 90% full almost all the time.
[1:14:25] So like you not doing whatever you expected or laid out for them to do. Dude, if that takes their stress bar to that next level or over the edge, there's going to be a lot of that stress getting projected at you and it's not going to be fun.
[1:14:45] You ideally can lower their stress bar, not increase their stress bar by accomplishing what you're supposed to accomplish. NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Discussão sobre garantias em ofertas - quando usar, quando não usar, problemas de mentalidade de perda, sinalização de baixo status
[1:15:40] How how do guarantees come into your offer in general? I sent you a DM as well, but maybe we can go through it. Like like what do you think like h how do you verbal uh verbalize your guarantee and like how do you think about that?
[1:16:00] Guarantees are relatively unnecessary unless the person is uncertain about what you're selling. So if if you find that there's still friction by the time you get to the close, then you can offer a guarantee as a closing strategy in order to remove the uncertainty that exists.
[1:16:20] But other than that, I'm not a big fan of the uh of uh guarantees because typically, to be fair, some people will think like a guarantee offers uh like comfort to the other party, you know, but what it typically does is it gets both parties focused on losing in going into the deal, which I'm not a fan of.
[1:16:45] And I do believe attributes to a higher probability of loss. Um, in addition to that, I'm also I'm also very uh I don't know. I'm very against the idea of signaling that you're willing to essentially work for free if you lose, you know?
[1:17:05] Like to me that signals low status. Not a fan of that. Like I'm not a charity, you know? And we we tell people that too if they ask like a role play with me, Kristoff. Say uh act like you're the client and act like you want a guarantee from me.
[1:17:25] Can I get a can I get a guarantee that this will work or how does it look like? Yeah, you know, just one quick question, Kristoff. Why are you focused on losing going into the deal?
[1:17:45] I don't know. I just want to like kind of like in case anything goes wrong, right? Like I just kind of like feel like, you know, that's always good. You promise a lot of things. So, you know, is there something you can kind of like guarantee in.
[1:18:05] You know, there are a few things I can guarantee. I can guarantee my performance. You know, I'll tell you what, if I fail on my end, I'll happily give you your money back because I won't want to be in the deal either.
[1:18:25] But, you know, in these kind of deals, Kristoff, like it's a give and take because I got to rely on you, too. I can't guarantee your performance. How can I guarantee your performance?
[1:18:50] That makes sense. I mean to be clear like you know help me understand like once again I'm happy to guarantee what I do. Are you willing to guarantee what you do? Like what happens if you fail? You gonna pay me double?
[1:19:10] No, that's good. Like look, I want to go into the deal and I want to focus on winning. Okay. I'd rather you have me ask questions like, "Hey man, is there anything that we haven't covered here today that can increase the probability that much further that we're going to win in this deal together?"
[1:19:30] You know, I don't want you asking questions like, "Well, what happens if we lose?" That's what fucking losers think. Losers think of losing. Winners think of winning. And I'm not just making that up as a sales tactic. Like, it's a real fucking lesson. Okay?
[1:19:50] I've seen one too many times that people like you that are focused on losing end up being losing deals. And it's not a shocker to me, you know? So, to be clear, I don't know. [1:20:15] Are you uncertain in yourself? Because to be clear, my certainty is as high as it needs to be on my end. I know I could do this for you. That's why I'm willing to do the deal in the first place.
[1:20:30] No, 100%. That's good. Yes. You see what I'm saying? Um Yeah. And it's true, right? [1:20:55] Ju just to be clear, like, you know, we're cool giving people their money back if we fuck up, you know, like that's fair. I'm not opposed to that, but it's like in most of these deals we do, it's like we're respons we're responsible for what we do and they're responsible for what they do.
[1:21:15] And if they fail on their end, like what the fuck. What am I supposed to do about that? You know, like am I am I the one that gets burned because you fucking shit the bed on your half of the deal? Like no.

[1:24:05] So as an example, like you know, if you keep selling to a $2,000 a month client, you have to work with five people for every one person that could pay $10,000. So it's like you literally have to do five times as much.

📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO
NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:
$41,000 - Audemars Piguet Royal Oak
$82,000 - Patek Philippe Nautilus considerado
$2-5 milhões - Tiffany Patek Nautilus
$500,000 - Richard Mille de Tom Brady
$20,000-$25,000 - Voo privado one-way Miami-NY
$2,000-$3,000 - First class round trip
$15,000/mês + 10% net rev share - Estrutura de pricing da agência
$7,500 - Tratamento de células-tronco
30 milhões - Células-tronco por tratamento
$23,000/mês - Gasto inicial com tratamento da mãe (5 meses)
$85 - Ovo poached em restaurante 2 estrelas Michelin
4 ovos - Servidos por noite no restaurante
$250,000/mês - Cliente de 24 anos por 3 anos
$3,500 - Cotação inicial de mudança (final price)
$2,200 - Cobrança adicional surpresa
$600 - Tip dado aos movedores
$10,000 - Expectativa inicial de custo de mudança
$5,000 - Aluguel de iate de 90 pés por 8 horas
18% - Tip sugerido para iate
13 guests - Máximo por lei da Florida
100+ - Diferentes quimioterapias testadas
1 mês - Tempo para eliminar câncer com tratamento personalizado
$25 - Leads qualificados para accredited investors
30 dias - Timeline para ROI inicial
6-12 meses - Timeline comum para $1M/mês
90% - Nível de stress bar de pessoas ricas constantemente
2021-2022 - Anos do boom da indústria de automação
3 Ferrari Portofinos + 1 California - No mesmo garage
4 investigadores privados - Colocados em cliente que não entregou
$150,000 - Dívida mencionada em nota no banheiro
7 pares - Sapatos testados na Chanel
40 minutos - Experiência de compra na Chanel
2 pares - Comprados finalmente
30 segundos - Tempo para trazer bebida na Chanel
$2,000 - Cliente típico de ticket baixo
$10,000 - Cliente de ticket alto (5x menos trabalho)
ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:
5 Categorias Principais para Ofertas a Ricos:
Making Money (mais próximo da geração direta de receita = mais valor)
Saving Time (transporte, velocidade de resultados, eficiência)
Status (sinalização social, conexão com pares, não vendível diretamente)
Quality (experiência em serviços, build quality em produtos)
Living Longer/Health (tratamentos personalizados, wellness clinics, age reversal)
Categorias Secundárias (Menor Prioridade):
Lower Stress
Have Fun
Love/Gifts/Pets
Estrutura de Oferta Completa:
What You Do (bullet points do serviço)
Pricing (sinaliza incentivos e alinhamento)
How You Break It Down (framework de 30 dias para ROI)
Expectations (categoria separada e crítica)
Pricing que Sinaliza Incentivos:
Rev share mostra alinhamento de interesses
Preço histórico vs. atual indica oportunidade de investimento
Estrutura month-to-month comunica confiança em resultados
Gerenciamento de Expectativas:
Expectativas conservadoras > overpromise
Timeline realista para grandes resultados (6-12 meses para $1M/mês)
Quebrar expectativas = falha percebida mesmo com sucesso real
Qualidade em Serviços:
Personalização através de histórico de compras
Zero sinais de frustração com requests
Antecipar necessidades antes do cliente pedir
Ser autêntico e personável apropriadamente
Messaging para Ricos:
Verbiage específica (liquid capital, predictable cash flow)
Ambiente onde ricos se encontram
Qualificadores explícitos de quem você quer
Algoritmo encontra demographic através de messaging correto
NÃO misturar messaging de ricos com pessoas normais (oil and water)
Status Não É Vendido - É Atribuído:
Status vem de quem usa/compra o produto
Exemplo Drake + Delilah restaurant
Qualidade extrema pode gerar status
Precisa polarização extrema vs. competição
Garantias:
Evitar a menos que haja incerteza no close
Foca ambas partes em perder
Sinaliza baixo status (trabalhar de graça)
Garantir apenas sua performance, não a do cliente
Stress Bar Concept:
Ricos operam em 90% stress constantemente
Objetivo é baixar stress, não adicionar
Quebrar expectativas aumenta stress = projeção negativa
ESTRUTURA:
Framework de 5 Categorias Principais
Offer Structure de 4 Componentes
Decision Matrix: Volume vs. High Ticket (matemática não opinião)
Transition Strategy para Rich People (não misturar demographics)
Consulting Offer para Rebranding (2-3 meses antes de hands-on)
DADOS FINANCEIROS:
Agency pricing: $15k/mês + 10% net rev share
30 dias para break-even ou 2:1 ROAS mínimo
6-12 meses para $1M/mês (timeline comum)
Alguns clientes: $1M em 30-90 dias (exceção)
Mason: Centenas de milhares/mês com demographic normal
Stem cell treatment: $7,500/tratamento, $23k/mês inicial
Moving scam: $3,500 quote → $5,700 real (ainda abaixo de $10k expectativa)
Private jet: $20-25k one-way vs. $2-3k first class round trip
Yacht rental: $5k base + 18% tip + deposit
Cancer treatment personalizado: Dezenas de milhares
Chanel experience: Alto ticket, 40 minutos, 7 tentativas sem frustração
DURAÇÃO: 1:37:50 FORMATO: Group coaching call / Masterclass sobre ofertas para pessoas ricas
